startScreenOptions.title: BI13.1-BI13.5 — Interactive Lecture (BI)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 4 embedded questions for BI13.1-BI13.5.

interactions.label: The narration describes the RAS gene as behaving like a 'light switch permanentl

params.question: The narration describes the RAS gene as behaving like a 'light switch permanently jammed on.' Which mechanism of oncogene activation accounts for this behaviour?

answers.text: Point mutation — a single nucleotide change locks the RAS protein in its active state

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. RAS is activated by a point mutation that alters a single nucleotide, locking the protein in its active (GTP-bound) conformation. This is why it behaves like a switch that cannot be turned off.

answers.text: Gene amplification — excess copies of the RAS gene overwhelm cellular growth controls

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Gene amplification produces too many copies of a gene, as seen with MYC in lymphomas and breast cancers. RAS activation typically involves a point mutation, not increased copy number.

answers.text: Chromosomal translocation — RAS is repositioned next to a constitutively active promoter

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Chromosomal translocation moves a gene to a new chromosomal location, potentially activating it under a different promoter. RAS is typically activated by a point mutation rather than translocation.

answers.text: Epigenetic modification — altered methylation patterns activate RAS transcription

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Epigenetic modification is a real mechanism of gene regulation but is not one of the three oncogene activation pathways discussed: point mutation, gene amplification, and chromosomal translocation.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review the three oncogene activation mechanisms: point mutation (single base change), gene amplification (extra copies), and chromosomal translocation (gene relocated). RAS activation is a classic example of a point mutation.
Well done. Point mutations in RAS are among the most common oncogenic events, found in roughly 30% of all human cancers.

interactions.label: A 62-year-old man has a mildly elevated PSA level. His clinician explains that t

params.question: A 62-year-old man has a mildly elevated PSA level. His clinician explains that this finding alone does not confirm prostate cancer. Which statement best explains this reasoning?

answers.text: PSA is only elevated in advanced-stage prostate cancer, not early disease

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. PSA can be elevated in both early and advanced prostate cancer. The real limitation is that PSA also rises in non-malignant conditions such as benign prostatic hyperplasia.

answers.text: PSA is primarily a marker for testicular cancer and is unreliable for prostate pathology

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. PSA is specific to prostate tissue, not testicular cancer. Testicular tumours are monitored using AFP and HCG. The issue is that PSA rises in benign prostate conditions, not that it targets the wrong organ.

answers.text: PSA can also be elevated in benign prostatic hyperplasia, so it must be interpreted alongside imaging and biopsy

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. No tumour marker is perfectly specific. PSA can rise in benign prostatic hyperplasia, so an elevated level must always be correlated with imaging and biopsy findings before a diagnosis of cancer is made.

answers.text: A single elevated PSA value is considered a normal physiological fluctuation with no clinical significance

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. While minor fluctuations occur, a persistently elevated PSA warrants investigation. The key point is that elevation is not cancer-specific — clinical correlation with imaging and biopsy is essential.

overallFeedback.feedback: Remember: no tumour marker is 100% specific. PSA can rise in benign conditions like BPH; CA-125 can rise in endometriosis. Markers guide investigation but never diagnose cancer alone.
Excellent. This principle applies to all tumour markers: PSA can rise in BPH, CA-125 in endometriosis. Always interpret markers in clinical context alongside imaging and histopathology.

interactions.label: Chronic alcohol consumption leads to an apparent increase in alcohol tolerance.

params.question: Chronic alcohol consumption leads to an apparent increase in alcohol tolerance. Which biochemical pathway is responsible for this metabolic adaptation?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. ADH is the main ethanol-metabolising enzyme but operates at a relatively fixed capacity. It is not the inducible pathway that accounts for metabolic tolerance.

answers.text: Induction of the hepatic cytochrome P450 microsomal pathway

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. The cytochrome P450 pathway (specifically CYP2E1) is inducible — the liver ramps up production of this enzyme with sustained alcohol intake. This increased metabolic capacity contributes to the apparent tolerance observed in chronic drinkers.
Incorrect. Catalase plays only a minor role in ethanol metabolism. It does not significantly contribute to the tolerance seen with chronic alcohol consumption.

answers.text: Enhanced aldehyde dehydrogenase (ALDH) converting acetaldehyde more rapidly

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. ALDH converts the toxic intermediate acetaldehyde to acetate in the second step of the pathway. It is not the enzyme whose induction explains metabolic tolerance to ethanol.

overallFeedback.feedback: The liver metabolises ethanol via three pathways: ADH (main, fixed capacity), cytochrome P450 (inducible — responsible for tolerance), and catalase (minor). The inducible nature of CYP450 is the key concept here.
Correct. Cytochrome P450 induction explains the metabolic component of alcohol tolerance — but this comes at a cost, as the pathway also generates more free radicals, contributing to liver damage.

interactions.label: AI-based autoverification in a clinical biochemistry laboratory checks each resu

params.question: AI-based autoverification in a clinical biochemistry laboratory checks each result against fixed universal reference ranges to flag abnormalities.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Incorrect. The narration explains that AI algorithms compare each result against the patient's history and expected biological variation — not fixed universal ranges. Additionally, AI helps establish population-specific reference ranges, which is essential in the Indian context where normal values can differ from Western populations.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct — the statement is false. AI autoverification compares each result against the patient's own history and expected biological variation, not fixed universal ranges. The narration also highlights that AI helps establish population-specific reference ranges, which is particularly important in India where normal values may differ from Western data.
